one_line_takeaway: A three-level hierarchical IoV intrusion-detection cascade (vehicle/RSU/edge/cloud) with Boruta+SHAP feature pruning hits 100% accuracy on CIC-IoV2024's DoS/spoofing classes using only 11-18 of 152 features, but the perfect scores on a single dataset and the merely-conceptual distributed deployment make overfitting and real-world scalability the key open questions.
key_finding: With Boruta+SHAP feature selection, the hierarchical RF (and LR) models reach 100% accuracy on the 2-class and 3-class tasks using only 11 features, and 100% on the 6-class task using 18 features (specifically the top-17 ranked features plus the 45th-ranked feature). At the third level with only 17 features the hard minority classes lag (GAS F1 = 80%, SPEED F1 = 88.89%, macro-avg F1 = 94.81%); adding the 45th feature lifts GAS to 99.97%, SPEED to 100%, and macro-avg F1 to 99.99% (weighted-avg F1 = 100%). This beats the CIC-IoV2024 originators Neto et al. (95% accuracy) and matches/exceeds other CIC-IoV2024 baselines while (the authors argue) using far fewer features than works that rely on all 152. The authors themselves flag the perfect scores as a legitimate overfitting concern.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a scalable three-level hierarchical classification framework for network intrusion detection in Internet of Vehicles (IoV) networks, contrasted against the conventional flat/centralized multiclass IDS. The framework's own contribution is: (1) a three-tier classifier cascade -- Level 1 binary (Benign vs Attack), Level 2 coarse-grained (Benign / DoS / Spoofing), and Level 3 fine-grained 6-class (Benign, DoS, and the four spoofing subtypes: Gas, RPM, Speed, Steering Wheel); (2) a proposal to physically distribute these classifiers across the IoV topology -- the root binary classifier on-board the vehicle, the Level-2 classifier at edge Roadside Units (RSUs), Level-3 classifiers at near-edge nodes, and the cloud reserved for (re)training; and (3) an ensemble feature-selection pipeline combining Boruta+Random Forest ranking with SHAP analysis to shrink the feature set (down to 11 features for 2-/3-class and 18 for 6-class, out of 152) so classifiers are light enough for resource-constrained edge nodes. NOTE: the multi-level hierarchical IDS idea itself is NOT novel to this paper -- Section II (Related Works) surveys prior two-level and three-level hierarchical IDS ([11]-[20]); the paper's specific novelty is applying/distributing a three-level hierarchy to IoV using the recent CIC-IoV2024 dataset and being (per the authors) among the first to attempt third-level (attack-subtype) classification on that dataset.
threat_model: Cyber threats against the IoV / in-vehicle CAN bus ecosystem, specifically Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks and spoofing attacks (with spoofing further broken into Gas, RPM, Speed, and Steering-Wheel spoofing). The abstract also motivates with malware, but the actual evaluated dataset (CIC-IoV2024) covers only DoS and spoofing on a real vehicle's CAN bus. The IDS is anomaly/signature-agnostic supervised classification of network traffic; the paper does NOT consider an adaptive/adversarial attacker (no evasion, poisoning, or adversarial-example evaluation), so the threat model is passive-traffic classification rather than adversary-in-the-loop.
limitations: (1) Perfect 100% accuracy across all levels -- including in the federated setting -- is a strong red flag for overfitting or data leakage; the authors acknowledge this and only mitigate it by pointing to gradual accuracy gains and 10-fold CV, without a held-out temporal/independent test or a second dataset. (2) Single dataset (CIC-IoV2024), which covers only DoS and spoofing on one 2019 Ford CAN bus, so generalization is untested (future-work promises CIC-IDS-2018). (3) The headline 'scalable distributed hierarchical IDS' is architectural/conceptual -- classifiers are only proposed to be deployed on vehicle/RSU/edge/cloud; the distribution is never actually run on real edge devices, and overhead numbers (13 KB memory, +0.13% response time) are analytical estimates, not measurements. (4) No adversarial-robustness, zero-day, or concept-drift evaluation despite motivating anomaly detection for novel threats. (5) The hierarchical model concededly uses more classifiers and higher time complexity than a flat model. (6) Class imbalance is severe (Gas = 0.81% of data) and handled only via stratified CV, not resampling.

datasets_used: CIC-IoV2024 (binary-feature version): 1,233,057 records total, 6 classes -- Benign 1,048,575 (85.04%), DoS 74,663 (6.06%), RPM spoofing 54,900 (4.45%), Speed spoofing 24,951 (2.02%), Steering-Wheel spoofing 19,977 (1.62%), Gas spoofing 9,991 (0.81%); 152 binary features derived from 1 ID feature + 8 data-byte features
quantitative_results: Root-level 2-class RF: 100% precision/recall/F1 for BENIGN and ATTACK with 11 features (macro-F1 97.58% with only 10 features)
Root-level 2-class LR: macro-F1 99.87% with 11 features vs 94.83% with 10 features
Second-level 3-class RF: 100% for BENIGN/SPOOFING/DoS with 11 features (macro-F1 97.36% at 10 features, dragged by SPOOFING recall 86.35%)
Third-level 6-class RF with 17 features: macro-avg F1 94.81%, weighted-avg F1 99.66%; GAS F1 = 80% (precision 66.67%), SPEED F1 = 88.89% (recall 80%)
Third-level 6-class RF with 18 features (17 + 45th): macro-avg F1 99.99%, weighted-avg F1 100%; GAS F1 = 99.97%, SPEED F1 = 100%
Overall accuracy (Table V): Proposed 100/100/100 for 2/3/6-class at 18 features (98.94% for 6-class at 10-11 features)
Federated learning (Flower, DNN, 10 clients, 5 rounds): 97.01/96.91/95.23 (2/3/6-class) at 10 features; 100/100/98.28 at 11 features; 100/100/100 at 18 features
Boruta+RF alone needs >=45 features for 100% third-level accuracy; adding SHAP reduces this to 18 (top-17 + 45th)
Estimated deployment overhead: model size 13 KB, response-time increase +0.13%, single-instance testing time 0.002 s
baselines_compared: Own flat (single multiclass) classifier counterpart at each level
Own federated-learning DNN variant (FedAvg via Flower)
Yagiz et al. [41] (KD-XVAE): 100/100/100 but used only attack data (benign excluded)
Mahdi et al. [42] (LSTM + XGB feature selection): 99.77% 6-class, spoofing-only
Aswal et al. [38] (CNN on decimal data): 99.99% but did not use full dataset
Gul et al. [39] (RF on decimal data): 99.64% 6-class
